description: Wednesday, 24th June 2026 | Social Services and Community Committee Disability Support Services Bill Hearing of evidence: Jonathan Godfrey Sharon Scurr Christine Middlemass Penelope Wood Pip Brunn Merryn Straker Peter Humphreys Marilyn Waring PSA Te Pukenga Here Tikanga Mahi Matthew Whiting Paul Singh Martin Roder James Edley Cherie Cawdron Disabled Older Persons Group Timothy Roland Stephen (Steve) Farrow Christian Hoerning Kate Beaumont Smith Daniel Clay India-Rose Clay Amy Clay New Zealand Disability Support Network Standards and Monitoring Services Alcohol Healthwatch Disabled Persons Assembly Spectrum Foundation Copyright (Parody and Satire) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: BusinessNZ Catherine Beard, Director of Advocacy It's in the Ballot Productions Sam Somers, Co-Chair New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Andrew Brown KC, Intellectual Property Law Committee member Earl Gray, Intellectual Property Law Committee member APRA AMCOS Anthony Healey, Head of APRA AMCOS Copyright licensing NZ Sam Irvine, Chief Executive Daniel Watterson, Contracts and Policy Manager
descriptionSummary: Disability Support Services Bill. Jonathan Godfrey. Sharon Scurr

chunks.text: Yeah. Good morning, Gary. Right. Good morning, and welcome to the Social Services and Community Select Committee. We are again hearing submissions today on the Disability Support Services Bill. And as said, yes, I'll just say it again for all those watching.

We look forward to hearing from submitters, and we're particularly interested as a committee in hearing three things if submitters wish to address those. It's one, what changes do you want to see in the bill? Two, what is your biggest concern? And three, what will your solution be? And just before we go away, I'll just go around quickly around the table, just so everyone gets a chance to say who they are and which area they represent.

So I'm Joseph Moody. I'm the member for Palmerston North and the chair of the committee. I'm Dana Cooper Patrick, the MP for East Coast and Deputy Chair of the committee. My name is Maureen Pugh. I am the MP for West Coast Tasman, and for low vision people, I've got short, brown hair. I'm wearing a black top with large white spots.

And my name is Laura McClure. I am an Act List MP based in Christchurch and a disability spokesperson, and I am wearing a pink blazer, and I've got medium length blonde hair. Hi, I'm Jamie Arbuckle, list MP for New Zealand First, living in the top of the south. And you're the good-looking one.

Morena tatou. My name is Marama Fox. I'm the Member of Parliament Maori for Te Tai Tokerau, and I've got large glasses on and white fluffy hair. Oh, fluffy. Well, kia ora e te whanau. Ko Kahurangi Carter toku ingoa. I am a Green Party list MP based in Christchurch Central.

I am the disability spokesperson for the Greens, and I have a white shirt which has a little bow tie and a black bow in my hair, and look forward to hearing from you all today. Thank you. Yes. And kia ora koutou. Ko Willow-Jean Prime toku ingoa. My name is Willow-Jean Prime.

I'm a Labor list MP based in Te Tai Tokerau, and I am Labor's spokesperson for social development and employment, and also for tamariki children. Kia ora. My name's Helen White. I'm the MP for Mount Albert. I have the portfolio for the prevention of family and sexual violence, and I also have a portfolio which is community and voluntary.

Thank you. Oh, good morning, Jonathan. Thank you for being with us this morning online. We have five minutes with you this morning. The time is yours. Welcome. Yeah. Thank you. Can I just check you can hear me? We certainly can. Yep. See and hear you loudly. Right. Lovely. Look, I'm actually coming in to you from Melbourne where I'm doing some work this week, and it's not lost on me that the disabled people of Australia have it a lot better off than the disabled people of New Zealand in terms of the dignity and respect that is offered to them by the way

supports are upholding and advancing their rights, freedoms in life. And I have to tell you that I really hope that that audio description, which isn't really much use for me, I hope that that is not the last respect that you show for disabled people in this process. Because this bill is a tail that is wagging a dog.

The tail is that Supreme Court decision and sections 12 onwards, and I have to tell you, I think there's a breach of faith with families, et cetera, that is wrapped up in that part of the bill. But that's just the tail that is wagging a dog, because there was no other bill in place at the moment for sections 12 to 16 to find a home.

But sections one to 11 are a dog. That is the worst piece of legislation that is anti-disabled people that has been put before Parliament in decades. It is completely counter to the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities that the government in 2008 ratified on behalf of New Zealand.

In 2008, a peg was put in the ground, we are going to do better by disabled people from this point onwards. It's my understanding, because I can't find anything in the public domain, that MPs have not been given all of the advice from officials that was necessary to tell you how bad sections one to 11 actually are.

They take the rights and freedoms of disabled people and actively strip them away and turn those rights and freedoms into a burden on our families. And I might not be a person in receipt of disability support services, but my dignity as a disabled person has been questioned as much by sections one to 11 as any of the 50-something thousand people who are in receipt of DSS today.

It's a breach of the convention with respect to Article 4.3, because no one thought to work with disabled people before this bill was introduced to the House. Don't worry about sections 12 to 16, but even sections one to 11 could have been, should have been, but was not socialized with disabled people, our representative organizations, or anyone actually out there in the real world who would give MPs the advice you should have received.

It's a breach of Articles 19 and 28, which is the core elements of making sure that we as disabled people get to live independently, exercise choice and control, and have an adequate standard of living. This bill is counter to those rights that the New Zealand government has already said are New Zealanders' rights.

That's what was done in 2008. And I invite you to ask officials from Whaikaha, from Justice, who has links with international treaties, why we have not seen anything in the public domain that questions sections one to 11 and informs you that you should not pass this bill.

I suggest to you that if you have any conscience whatsoever about the rights and freedoms of disabled people to live a dignified life alongside our non-disabled peers, you will reject this bill in its entirety. If you have any humanity, you will reject this bill.

If you want to ever be able to say in future that you respect disabled people, you will reject this bill. And I am very, very sure that you will be hearing messages like that from various people who are from the slightly affected through to the fundamentally, "This is going to ruin my life."

I'm very happy to take questions, but I think the message I'm putting to you is very clear. It's a dog. Toss it out and actually be humans that can show their faces in New Zealand for having respect for all Kiwis, whether we are disabled or not. Thank you for your time this morning.

Leave questions. Thank you, John. Thank you, John. We didn't leave enough time for questions, but thank you for making a submission and sharing your views this morning. Thank you. Good luck for the rest of your day. Thank you. All right. Our next submitter is Sharon, also via Zoom. Morning, Sharon.

Just because of the old mute. There we go. Yep, no, I got you now. Thank you. All right. Welcome. We have five minutes with you this morning as well. The time is yours. Thank you. My name is Sharon, and I'm here because of my 15-year-old son, Cantby. My son has mild intellectual disability, autism, and ADHD. His greatest challenge is not his willingness to learn, contribute, participate in society. His greatest challenge is navigating systems that he'll never be able to fully understand independently.

My concern about this bill is not theoretical. It is based on lived experience. Over many years, my family repeatedly sought help through education and health systems. Too often support existed on paper but was unavailable in practice. I removed my son from school, not because I rejected support, but because the support he needed was not available.

We paid for assessments ourselves. We became my son's teacher, advocate, coordinator, caregiver, because the support he needed was not available when he needed it. At times, I felt I spent so much time fighting the system and filling the gaps, I had less time to simply be his mom. That experience is why I'm concerned about the provisions that assume support can simply be provided elsewhere. The reality is that the disabled people can be left caught between systems while families carry the consequences.

My other concern about the ministerial powers within the bill. My concern is not that ministers have power. My concern is that the future changes. Sorry. The future changes to the eligibility, criteria, funding setting, purchasing rules, and support arrangements could be made through program or regulatory changes with limited safeguards for people whose lives will be affected. As parents of disabled children, we're left with the question that never goes away.

What will happen to our children when we are no longer here? Most days we focus on the next step, the next skill, sorry, the next milestone, because if we spend too much time thinking about the future, the fear can become overwhelming. But we cannot ignore the future either. For the past five to six years, I've worked every day to help my son develop literacy, education, communication, life skills, confidence, and independence after removing him from school.

Like many parents, everything I do is focused on helping my child build foundation for a meaningful adult life. That is why the future stability of disability supports matter so much. Support is not a cost. Support is an investment in independence, security, participation in the community.

The right support creates opportunity, participation, and independence. Every support removed today has the potential to reduce opportunities tomorrow. Families provide invaluable support, but families cannot be expected to replace disability support systems. Families should complement disability support systems, not replace them. Supporting disabled people should be a shared responsibility between families, community, and government, not a burden that's progressively transferred onto those already carrying the greatest load.

My son cannot read this bill, understand the future program changes, analyze the regulatory decisions, or advocate for protections he may need as an adult. One day I will not be here to do that for him. I ask the committee to judge this bill not only in how it affects disabled people today, but how it will protect disabled people when their parents, caregivers, and advocates are no longer able to speak on their behalf. Thank you, and I welcome your questions.

Thank you, Sharon. What would you say to the contention that the bill doesn't reduce or remove any existing support, but rather it makes a transparent legal framework for the way DSS already operates? Well, the framework in theory does that, but there is no security safeguards. There's no regulations included to show that that's what's going to happen. So it's a good faith until you've put the rules into the system. So what we're asking for is please put the rules in the system before you create it, rather than expecting us to believe that

you will put the rules in in good faith. And it's hard to believe that when 18 months ago you just decided to put a whole stop to things, and that sits with the fear of parents. Like, we're left trying to... I believe he's wanting to stabilize the system. I believe in good faith that is what your intentions are. But what's the intentions in 5 years' time, in 10 years' time?

The whole point of the act is to make sure there's protections for everybody. Sup. Also sup or sup. Yeah, just to coax up on that. I definitely hear the concerns about the secondary legislation or the regulations, but we do have a ministry of regulation around- Regulation making powers, and there will be a way in which people can contest regulation.

And the other thing is, it will still be subject to a cabinet approval, any type of form of regulation, and that is quite common with any type of secondary legislation. So I'm just wanting to make sure that you're aware of that as well. But will any of those conversations include the people in the disability community? Yes.

Yes, they should do. Yeah, 100%. Yeah. Shouldn't it? No. So why wasn't it done before? Why wasn't it done now? That's kind of the question that's being asked. Everything's being done after the fact, rather than if all of this can be done, why we haven't advocated for it to be done before the bill goes through.

Can I just comment on that? After, yeah. I'll just comment on that very quickly. The minister has advised there were 18 months of consultation with the disability community to stabilize the support system and inform the principles of the bill. So there was consultation with the community. Perhaps not to the level and extent that you might- It wasn't the job being asked.

I appreciate- That's true... I absolutely appreciate that that submission was done. I submitted myself, but none of that was asked about this. And I think that needs to be really clear. We all want a framework. We all want security put in place. Nobody's disagreeing that. But how it's being done is not what we were wanting.

We were wanting more of a let's work together and make sure these frameworks and the rules that yous are setting within the frameworks are actually achievable and workable, rather than creating something. Like for example, my own situation with the education system. It implies that if funding can be found within another system, my son would not get funding. There is not enough funding within the education system to fund my son.

So where does that leave the system? There's no guarantees written within what you have to make sure that doesn't happen. Okay. Well, thank you, Sharon. Appreciate you making a submission this morning, sharing your lived experience with the committee. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you for everything you're doing. Yeah. Thank you.
The next speaker is Christine, also via Zoom.

Morning, Christine. Can see you, and looks like you're on mute now. So welcome to the committee. We have five minutes with you this morning, so the time is yours. Thank you. I am immensely proud of our 19-year-old son. He's the most courageous person I will ever know. I have never thought of him as a burden. The proposed legislation will change this, making him a burden on us as parents, his only sibling, and our extended family, or we will not be able to cope and he will end up in care.

What a choice. People like my son are the most vulnerable members of society. A perpetual toddler reliant on others for their every need. Instead of protecting him and others like him, this legislation discriminates against them and their families and whanau. The proposed legislation creates the potential for a pass the parcel scenario as family members burn out.

There is no person-centric self-determination for the person with the additional needs or disabilities or their families. Our choices will be more limited and not fit for purpose. You are looking at passing legislation that affects approximately 1.6 million members of society, roughly 30% of the population.

The lack of publicity, transparency, consultation and co-design is abhorrent. The Enabling Good Lives principles introduced by a national government in collaboration with the disability community are nonexistent in the proposed legislation. I cannot believe that our current government is introducing such a fiscally irresponsible piece of legislation, which has the potential for huge costs to the country, both direct and indirect.

By introducing means testing, existing supports for some families will disappear. The burnout for those families not receiving ongoing supports will ensure more demand for residential care homes. This is by far the most expensive model available. Close the legal loopholes which have led to the recent court rulings making MSD an employer of some family carers. But I would urge you to stop the rest of this legislation proceeding until proper consultation and co-design with those directly affected.

If not, remove the following areas of the proposed legislation: ministerial programs, means testing, discrimination against family or whanau carers. This legislation and individualized funding rules should not preclude family or whanau members from being paid carers, as they are usually the people who know how to care for the person in the best way and have their best interests at heart. Until proper consultation can take place, for those who need comprehensive care, implement a cap for live-in carer employee payments through individualized funding to the

current maximum of 40 hours for family members. This should be regardless of the relationship to the person with the additional needs or disabilities. For these persons, additional supports for respite, personal cares, vocational, et cetera, should remain in place to assist the carer and avoid burnout. Nationally standardized assessment based on need is positive, ensuring all aspects of a person's life are realistically assessed.

You could introduce external verification or audits in various settings, and that would assure that this process is robust. If this legislation goes into effect in its current form, it will break our family, and we are a really resilient family. If it breaks us, it will have created carnage across society for people such as our son and for all their families and whanau.

Thank you. Thank you, Christine. Just on the income and asset testing provisions in the bill, are you aware that the bill doesn't change the current income and asset testing provisions, which have long been used in a limited number of DSS supports? Then why is it in there? Reflecting existing practice.

Then that needs to be clarified. It doesn't actually stipulate that it's only based on... So what we're talking about, the bill talks about the fact that family and whanau carers, and some of the discussion documents created by MSD, that family and whanau should not be being paid carers. That's just not going to fly. You are going to break families.

But I think that's the difference between being an employee of MSD- Mm... and being a paid carer. Which I think, with the funding model, they still get paid- Correct... but they're not employees of MSD. There's a difference. The money is increasing. That's right. To support the family. Yeah. Karori. Thank you. Thank you for your submission, Christine, and sharing the story of your son and your whanau.

As opposition MPs, we have been told that we are fear-mongering around the changes to the DSS primary legislation. What do you say to that? Well, I'm really pleased that you are actually sticking up for us because of the lack of publicity around all of this, we're not even aware if the opposition parties are even going in to bat for us. No, you're not fear-mongering.

This legislation, I agree with the first submitter, this is such a dog of a piece of legislation. It should never have been written. It's really badly put together, and there's no real detail in it. So until there's actual proper co-design and detail across the board with all the people affected, then no. Please keep sticking up for us.
No. Thank you, Christine. Can we just summarize? Yeah. Couple of last things. Yeah. It's unmuted.
All right. Our next submitter is Penelope. Penelope. Sorry.

Kia ora. Kia ora. Welcome. We have finance with you this morning, so the time is yours, fire away when you're ready. Thank you. Mōrena, and thank you for giving me the opportunity to speak today. My name is Penelope Wood, and I'm a full-time carer and mother of three children. Our whanau is busy, diverse, and loving.

My eldest child, Abby, is 11. She is autistic, inventive, and loves animals. She requires support to have her needs met both at school and at home. Our second child, Ella, is eight. She's full of energy, loves music, dance, books, netball. She does not have a disability, but she grows up in a home where disability shapes our lives.

Our youngest child is Finn. Sorry. He's five years old. Finn is beautiful, determined, and has a gorgeous smile. He attends Te Whitianga specialist school in Dunedin. Finn required urgent care after birth and was transferred from Dunedin to Starship within 24 hours. Genetic testing revealed he has a very rare genetic syndrome.

Finn has profound and complex care needs. He is non-verbal, does not walk, is tube-fed, is partially deaf, and has multiple seizures daily, some requiring medical intervention and ambulance support. Developmentally, he's around eight months old. Our lives are built around his care, medication, feeding, personal cares, appointment, equipment, and being his voice in every space.

Finn will need lifelong care. My husband and I work as a team. My husband continues to work in a very demanding job, but he also provides significant care outside of work hours as well. We decided early on that I'd become the full-time carer so we could meet all of our children's needs safely and sustainably. I am asking today for two critical changes to Clauses 8 and 11, plus everything else of the bill to change, really.

I ask that Clause 8 is amended so that unpaid family care, natural supports, or other resources are not treated as something that must come first before funded disability support is available. Families will always provide love, advocacy, and care. But for families supporting someone with complex and lifelong disability, the level of care required can go beyond what one household and extended family can sustainably provide without support.

Individualized funding has been incredibly important for our whanau because it gives us flexibility. It allows us to choose support that works for Finn and our family. We have one trusted person who provides respite. She understands Finn, her stages, and our family. This consistency and trust are extremely valuable.

Being able to use funding flexibly has also allowed me to be recognized for the care I provide. I stepped away from my career to care for Finn. Being paid, even at a minimum level, has given me dignity and recognition that my work has value. This is not about replacing family love with payment.

It's about acknowledging the reality of disability care. Secondly, Clause 8. I ask that Clause 8 is amended so that future changes to the disability support system cannot be introduced without proper parliamentary debate and meaningful consultation with disabled people and their whanau. Funding stability is essential.

Families like ours need to plan for the future, for equipment, transport, care needs, and what life will look like as our children grow. When funding changes suddenly, like it has, and this bill seems to want to create more uncertainty and instability in that field, we should not have to repeatedly tell our hardest stories to prove that our children still need support.

The solution I would like to see is one clear source of funding, one consistent system, and one pathway for disabled people and carers to access support. We need predictable, flexible, and secure supports. Families like ours are often isolated and highly vulnerable, not because we lack strength, but because the demands of disability can be enormous.

The disability system should reduce that burden, not add to it. Please protect choice, dignity, flexibility, and stability for disabled people and their whanau. Nga mihi. Mihi. Oh, thank you, Panapali. Yeah. Just on the, we've got a question from me on the, you section-- Clause 8, which is around, the bill in terms of family support. Are you aware that it reflects the current practice of DSS, which has been in place for a long time to consider supports already provided by the family, but it doesn't require family to

be the primary or the only source of support? I think this bill puts it-- It gives a gray area. It seems to present a picture that we shall be dedicated 100% to our family and provide that without the support. Whether that's been in the past or not, but this feels like we're entrenching it and saying that you shall reach the bottom of the system.

Support is there, but only at the bottom of the cliff. That's what it feels like to me from my lived experience and from reading this bill and what you're proposing. Look, thank you for making a submission, and thank you for sharing the story of your family and of the work you do looking after your family. Kia ora, Panapali. Thank you for everything you do.

All right, our next submitter is Pip, also via Zoom. Welcome, Pip. We can see you. It looks like you've got yours off mute. So look, we've got you for five minutes this morning. Welcome. Good morning. Thank you. I'm here with a group of friends and disabled people here in Palmerston North. My name is Pip. Today I'm speaking as a parent, a disability advocate, and a member of the MidCentral Regional Enabling Good Lives leadership group.

I'm not focusing today on the clauses of the bill. I'm focusing on the human consequences of this bill. You've heard many people say the same things in different ways. I've sat in meetings for almost three decades contributing to the same conversations, searching for hope and continued improvement of support for disabled people and their families. We thought that we were getting there.

In 2017, the National Government primarily mandated the Enabling Good Lives approach. And when EGL came to our region, I was very, very cautious, knowing that I had already been part of many promises in the past to improve the way support is provided for disabled people. We have since then made so much progress, but this bill undoes all of that work.

We know that this government has invested a significant cost to improving services and support, but this is not just about the financial cost. So I recognize that you have heard us, but please listen and please understand and please use our voices to create what is happening next. Significant consultation did occur, but none of this consultation included anything that was in this bill or that this bill was even going to be created.

We know that the minister- ... who agreed to this bill think that it is right and they think that it is okay. But what is that saying for the ethical, cultural, and conscious beliefs of people who think it is okay to completely ignore the basic human rights of our most vulnerable people? I can't speak for every disabled person.

Disabled people are not one group with one experience. But I can share what I have heard repeatedly over many years. People want to be trusted, heard, and genuine choice and control over their lives. For decades, disabled people and families have worked alongside governments to build a different future, a future based on enabling good lives, and where disabled people are recognized as citizens and not burdens. As experts in our lives, as people with aspirations, relationships, talents, and contributions. We fought for choice, control, and

trust, and we believe we were finally making progress. That is why this bill has generated such concern. You have asked what my biggest concern is. My biggest concern is the shift in power this bill creates. The power moves away from disabled people and whanau. Power moves back towards systems, bureaucracy, and control.

As both a parent and a regional leadership group member, I hear the same message repeatedly. People want partnership, not paternalism. They want to be involved in decisions that affect their lives. They do not want decisions made about them without them. Many people and families already feel afraid.

Afraid to challenge decisions, afraid to speak honestly, afraid that advocating for themselves may come at a cost. Disability support system should be built on trust, partnership, and respect, not fear. You have asked me what changes I want to see. I want to see disabled people's choice and control clearly protected in legislation.

I want support to be based on disability-related needs, not assumptions about what families can provide. I want enabling good lives principles embedded in the legislation in a meaningful and enforceable way. I want disabled people and whanau to remain genuine partnerships in decisions that affect their lives, and I want a future government's prevented from significantly changing support through regulation without meaningful consultation and co-design with disabled people.

But I also want to acknowledge another important truth. Not every disabled person has experienced family as a safe place. Some have experienced harm from family members. Others have experienced harm from support workers, services, and institutions that have been entrusted with their care. Their experiences matter.

That is why the answer is not forcing disabled people to rely on family, and it is not forcing disabled people to rely on strangers. The answer is choice, real choice, choice that belongs to the disabled person. You have asked me what my solution is, and my solution is simple. Build a bill that trusts disabled people, protects supported decision-making, protects the right of disabled people to choose who supports them. For some of that will be family, and some of that it won't.

But both choices deserve equal respect. As children become adults, they should be supported to develop their own voice, their own preferences, and their own choices. A good disability support service bill should protect that right. Committee members, this is not a resistance to change.

We've had so much change over the years. We are exhausted. This isn't resistance. This is grief. This is grief over the decades of advocacy that have been slipping away. Grief that trust is being replaced by control, grief that partnership is being replaced by bureaucracy. Before you make decisions, I ask that you think about the disabled people who cannot stand before you today. I am lucky.

I have people in this room with me. And think about the people sitting around kitchen tables worrying about the future of their own families and their own children. And think about the communities that have invested years and years of helping build a more inclusive Aotearoa. At the end of the day, this bill is not about funding. It is about people. It is about dignity.

It is about citizenship. It is about whether disabled people in Aotearoa have the right to live good lives and direct their own futures. Please don't take us backwards, because that is what this bill will do. All right. Thanks, Pip. Thank you for making that submission, and thanks to everyone who's with you there this morning supporting you.
So yeah. All right. Excellent. No, cheers. All right. Our next submitter will be Peter, also via Zoom. I'll try and adjust this.
I know. Technology and all its spoils. Yeah. That's good. Too much.

That's fine. Morning, Peter. We can't quite hear or see you at the moment. I'm clicking on my video and it's not happening. Oh, no. No worries. We can hear you. Could I just say just at the outset, just as Members of Parliament, we can't consider active court proceedings.

But look, other than that, we look forward to hearing from you. Okay. And mine's pretty basic. I've put mine in the written submission. First of all, I'd just like to thank you all for being there today and to listen to our submissions and to listen to what we want to say. So yeah.

What would I say? There's legal experts that can argue the Bill of Rights stuff and argue the human rights stuff that's happening and there's experts on that. But for me, it's about my daughter. It's about also what I've been through for the last 20 years. I was one of the plaintiffs in the Atkinson case, and we were successful in three courts. And finally, in 2012, we got a decision to say that we were employee, we were allowed to be paid. So under urgency in 2014-- or 2013 rather, Parliament rushed PAP4A through.

So they stopped people, stopped us from doing certain things, and it prevented some of our human rights activities and stuff like that. So that went on for quite a few years, and then in 2020, it was repealed by the Labor government, and then we had the new system that came in.

And I started the court case around 2019. So I've been involved with it for about 20 years and then been successful recently as an appellant in the Supreme Court with Christine God. Sorry, I'm a bit nervous here. Oh, no. With Christine. So it's taken us a long time, and I've been listening this morning about people being told that they were consulted.

I've been involved in this case all the way through. I'm a social worker, a qualified social worker. I've never been approached other than legally about any of this stuff. It's all been around case law, about taking us to court, about fighting the next case. There's never been a plan B for 20 years. And suddenly we've got to the end of the Supreme Court and they're saying now, "Oh, we're going to pass this new bill that is very similar to the 4A bill."

It's only not done under urgency, but under a different source of urgency that we have to get everything in very quickly. And, so yeah, it's almost like deja vu for me. Yeah, I would have liked somebody to come and talk, and I would have liked somebody to go and talk to the 40 people of what- however the numbers are, that were waiting at ERA, waiting for this decision- Mm-hmm... in the Supreme Court. They just waited for years for the decision, and now they've lost all their rights to actually go through the courts if this bill goes through.

So, around that, yeah, so that really concerns me that there's been discussion because I have not been involved with it, and I've been on the frontline of it. Mm-hmm. Just a little bit about Sian. She's extremely high needs. She's 37 years old. She goes to a day service, and we have a really good service where they take her out, they take her to swimming, and they take her in the community and stuff, just like we do as parents. Every second week we have a respite break, and she goes away. I drop her off at 3:00 on a Friday afternoon, and I pick her up on the Sunday afternoon,

and she sits in that house all those hours. She doesn't do any activities. That's her respite service. Somebody mentioned primary. We're not the primary or only source of support. That's the support that happens in our respite. She just stays there. She does absolutely nothing. She's a real social being. She loves being out with people. She loves going to events.

So that's one of the reasons we have never looked at residential care. We want to do it ourself, and the government is quite willing to pay for that type of service, but they're not willing to pay for parents to look after their children. So, it surprises me. There should be inspections of these places anyway.
